title: Lambdas 🐑
description: Discover Ruby's anonymous functions and their strict rules.

slides.title: The Sibling 👯
slides.content:
Procs have an almost identical twin sibling called a **Lambda**.

A Lambda is exactly identical to a Proc. It is just a block of code saved to a variable name so that it can be reused.

They both even use `.call` to run!

slides.title: Creating a Lambda
slides.content:
You create a Lambda essentially the same way you create a Proc, just using the word `lambda`.

```ruby
# Normal Proc
my_proc = Proc.new { puts "I am a Proc!" }

# Lambda
my_lambda = lambda { puts "I am a Lambda!" }
```

slides.title: Calling a Lambda
slides.content: They do the exact same thing! You call them using `.call` and you pass them into iterators using `&`.
slides.codeSnippet:
greet = lambda { |name| puts "Hello #{name}!" }

# Calling it directly
greet.call("Alice")

# Passing it to an iterator
["Bob", "Charlie"].each(&greet)

quizData.question: In 90% of situations, a Lambda acts exactly identical to what other Ruby feature?
quizData.explanation: They are functionally twins. They both store anonymous blocks of code inside variables so they can be reused.

slides.title: The Stabby Lambda 🗡️
slides.content:
Because typing out the word `lambda` every time is tedious, Ruby created a very popular shorthand version called the **Stabby Lambda**.

You replace the word `lambda` with a dash and an arrow `->` followed by curly braces, completely mimicking the look of arrow functions in JavaScript!

`my_lambda = -> { puts "Stab!" }`

slides.title: Stabbing in Action
slides.content: This is how all modern professional Ruby code writes lambdas! If your lambda requires a parameter, you put it in parentheses right after the stabby arrow.

quizData.question: Which of these is the correct syntax for a Stabby Lambda that takes the parameter `age`?
quizData.explanation: The dash and arrow point to the parameters wrapped in parentheses: `->(age) { }`
